themes.heading: Latest update
themes.text: Still current at:25 July 2026 Updated:15 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in Guinea. Higher levels apply in some areas. There's ongoing political and social tensions, including in Conakry. The situation may deteriorate without warning. Border closures, curfews and other movement restrictions may occur. Fuel and water shortages occur regularly. Ensure that you maintain enough food, water and emergency supplies (see 'Safety'). The Australian High Commission in Accra has very limited capacity to provide consular assistance in Guinea. We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Guinea overall due to the risk of civil unrest. Higher levels apply in some areas. Download Guinea Download Africa

themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: There's ongoing political and social tensions, including in Conakry and other towns. The situation may deteriorate without warning. Border closures, curfews and other movement restrictions may occur. Fuel and water shortages occur regularly. Ensure that you maintain enough food, water and emergency supplies. The border regions are dangerous. Do not travel to all areas along the border with Mali due to the threat of banditry, terrorist attacks and kidnapping. Armed robbery, conflict, and cross-border military and rebel activity is common in these regions. Violent protests have taken place previously, resulting in casualties. Avoid protests and public gatherings and remain vigilant. Follow the advice of local authorities. Monitor local media for updates. There's a risk of violent crime. Keep car windows up and doors locked, even when moving. Stay alert after dark or if there's a power outage. There's an increasing risk of foreign nationals being kidnapped along the border areas with Mali. Foreign nationals and foreign interests, including mining operations, may be targeted. Internet scams often originate in West African countries. Be wary of online contact from people you don't know. Don't send money until you've checked who you're sending it to. Don't travel to Guinea to get your money back or to get revenge. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Yellow fever, malaria, dengue, Zika virus, filariasis and other insect-borne diseases are common. Make sure your accommodation is insect-proof. Use insect repellent. HIV/AIDS is common. Take precautions if engaging in high-risk activities. There's an outbreak of mpox in parts of Africa, including Guinea. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone who has mpox. If there is a serious outbreak of infectious disease, follow the advice of local authorities. Medical and emergency services are very limited. You may need to pay cash up-front, even in an emergency. If you're seriously ill or injured, you'll need medical evacuation. Make sure you have the right insurance and enough money to cover all your costs. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: Local prison conditions are harsh, often without regular food and water. Pre-trial detention is common and can last for many months. Always carry original ID documents with you. Police and law enforcement can ask to see them. Serious crimes like murder and armed robbery can attract the death penalty. Penalties for drug offences are severe and include prison sentences in local jails. Same-sex relationships are illegal. Fines or jail sentences may apply. You can't export precious stones from Guinea without a licence from the relevant Guinean authorities. If you can't show a licence, you may be jailed. It's illegal to take photos or videos of military or government buildings. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: Contact the Embassy of the Republic of Guinea for details about visas, currency, customs and quarantine rules. Authorities may close borders at short notice. Border closures, curfews and other restrictions on your movement may occur. Air travel may be cancelled at short notice or borders closed in response to political unrest. Contact your airline for more information. You need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter and exit Guinea. Road travel can be dangerous, especially after dark. Robberies, accidents and fuel shortages are common. Fuel and water shortages occur regularly. You should maintain emergency supplies. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. Australia does not have an embassy or consulate in Guinea. For consular help, contact the Canadian Embassy in Senegal. You can also get help from the Australian High Commission in Ghana. The Australian High Commission in Accra currently has very limited capacity to provide consular assistance in West Africa, except in Ghana. To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission's social media accounts. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
themes.text: There's ongoing political and social tension, including in the capital Conakry. There's potential for the situation to deteriorate without warning. There's a high risk of violence during general strikes. In the event of a strike, shops could close for long periods of time with little warning. Ensure that you maintain stores of food, water and emergency supplies, sufficient to last at least three to four days. Disruptions to communication networks and internet service can occur with little notice, especially during periods of civil unrest. Telecommunications and internet services, including social media have been restricted by the government previously. The Australian High Commission in Accra has very limited capacity to provide consular assistance in Guinea.

themes.heading: Demonstrations and protests
themes.text: Security forces may use tear gas and fire warning shots to disperse crowds of protesters. Public gatherings and demonstrations can occur and may turn violent with little or no notice. Violent protests have occurred previously, resulting in casualties. Vehicles may be attacked while attempting to pass demonstrations. Criminals are known to take advantage of the resulting traffic congestion to rob drivers, passengers, and pedestrians. Demonstrations and strikes may disrupt goods and basic services, including road transport and international flights. To stay safe during periods of unrest: avoid protests, street rallies and public gatherings leave an affected area as soon as it's safe follow the advice of local authorities remain vigilant, monitor the media for updates, safety and security risks keep water and food supplies to last several days always have access to cash, medication and a first-aid kit keep your car fuelled and phone charged

themes.heading: Crime
themes.text: Crime happens in Conakry and other parts of Guinea. This includes: assaults armed robbery, hold-ups and muggings break-ins and burglars may be armed minor theft carjacking The risk of violent incidences can happen anytime but increases after dark. Crimes may be perpetrated by persons wearing military or police uniforms who are armed. Foreigners are often the target of crime, especially at the airport. If you plan to arrive in Conakry on a flight after dark, arrange your airport transfer before you travel. When travelling outside of Conakry, try to complete all travel before nightfall. Petty crime, such as pickpocketing and purse-snatching, occurs at the airport, in markets, taxis or communal transport, and near popular hotels and restaurants. Be aware of unsolicited offers of assistance from individuals who may be trying to steal luggage, handbags or wallets. Frequent power outages may worsen security conditions, especially in large urban centres. This often leads to more crime, especially if street and traffic lights fail. To protect yourself from crime: be vigilant while driving, especially when travelling to and from Conakry International Airport keep car doors locked and windows up at all times, even when moving exercise caution at the airports. Arrange to arrive during the day and be met by reliable contacts keep your passport and valuables in a secure place keep valuables and mobile phones out of sight ensure your accommodation has appropriate security arrangements stay alert after dark or if there's a power failure don't walk alone at night

themes.heading: Scams and fraud
themes.text: There have been reports of police extorting cash from foreigners and Guineans. Local businesses may lodge false complaints with police about foreigners in an attempt to extract additional payments. Commercial scams are on the rise. There are reports of trading scams involving diamonds, gold export and gold certification. This trade attracts criminal gangs and can involve kidnapping and extortion. Internet scams may come in the form of romance, friendship, business and employment opportunities. These scams often originate in West African countries. If you travel to West Africa as a scam victim, you are at risk of: financial loss kidnapping assault robbery murder This includes travelling to meet a friend or love interest, as well as trying to get your money back or get revenge. Commercial and relationship scams are common fraud schemes. In commercial scams, criminals ask for details of safe bank accounts to transfer large amounts of money. They may also: promise a percentage of this amount to you send fake cashier cheques for urgent goods shipments request large fees for a fake government contract extort money from you for a bogus business opportunity In relationship scams, criminals often meet their victims through internet dating sites or chat rooms. Once they have formed a connection, they may: ask you to send money so they can come to Australia ask you to come to Africa to meet them Once you transfer money, the relationship often ends. If you travel to Guinea to meet the criminals, you may be in danger. To protect yourself from scams: be wary of online contact from people you don't know don't send money to anyone until you've thoroughly checked them If you suspect a scam, get legal advice. Don't travel to Guinea to get your money back or to get revenge. You could be in danger.

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping occurs across the world with political, ideological, and criminal motives. Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas whilst travelling. Kidnaps can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. The risk of foreign nationals being kidnapped along the border areas with southern Mali is increasing. Foreign nationals and foreign interests, including mining operations, may be targeted. If, despite our advice, you travel to an area with a high risk of kidnapping our ability to provide consular assistance in these destinations is extremely limited. You’re responsible for your own safety. You can find more information on how to reduce your risk of kidnapping here: Kidnapping The Australian Government's longstanding policy is that it doesn't make payments or concessions to kidnappers. Ransom payments to kidnappers have funded further terrorist attacks and criminal activity. Ransom payments to terrorist groups are likely to contravene Australian counter-terrorism financing laws. More information: Reducing the risk of kidnapping Scams that affect travellers Reducing the risk of piracy

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: Terrorism is a threat worldwide. An attack could occur at any time, including places frequented by expatriates and foreigners. Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places. Terrorists may target hotels, beaches and restaurants. Attacks have happened in West Africa, particularly in: Mali Burkina Faso Cote d'Ivoire If there’s a terrorist incident, leave the area if it’s safe to do so and follow the instructions of local authorities.

themes.heading: Border region
themes.text: Instability in neighbouring countries and armed banditry in West Africa pose a risk. Expect an increased military presence in border areas. Reconsider your need to travel near the borders with Sierra Leone, Liberia and Côte d’Ivoire due to cross-border military and rebel activity, and tensions between ethnic groups that can turn violent. Do not travel to the region bordering Mali as there is a heightened risk of terrorist activity due to the presence of armed groups and militias. Clashes between armed groups and security forces could take place in the area. More information: Terrorist threats

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: The wet season is from May to October. In flooding, unsealed roads may become impassable without warning. The following essential services can also be affected: transport and infrastructure electricity distribution water and food supply emergency services Take caution and seek local advice. Monitor local media for up-to-date information on weather conditions and border crossing status prior to travel. Follow the advice of local authorities if extreme weather occurs. Register for alerts from the Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System.

themes.heading: Travel insurance
themes.text: Get comprehensive travel insurance before you leave and closely read the Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) for what is and isn’t covered. It must cover all medical costs overseas, including evacuation. The Australian Government will not pay for these costs. If you can’t afford travel insurance, you shouldn’t travel. This is true for everyone, even if you are healthy and fit. If you don’t have insurance, you might have to pay thousands of dollars up front for medical care. Before you travel, check your policy, make sure: you know what activities and medical care your insurance covers, including evacuations your insurance covers you for your whole trip, including any stopovers you know any exclusions – for example, you might need to pay extra for activities like motorcycling, snow sports, scuba diving or bungee jumping. More information: Travel insurance

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Ebola virus disease
themes.text: Guinea has experienced outbreaks of Ebola virus disease in the past. Protect yourself against the disease by practising good hygiene.

themes.heading: HIV/AIDS
themes.text: HIV/AIDS is common. Take precautions to reduce your risk of infection.

themes.heading: Insect-borne diseases
themes.text: Yellow fever is widespread. Yellow fever is a potentially fatal virus spread by mosquitoes. It's prevented by vaccination. Get vaccinated before you travel (see the 'Travel' section for vaccination requirements). Malaria is also widespread, along with filariasis, dengue, zika virus and other insect-borne diseases. To protect yourself from disease: make sure your accommodation is insect-proof use treated mosquito nets use insect repellent wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing consider taking medicine to prevent malaria Get medical advice if you have a fever, muscle pain, rash or severe headache.

themes.heading: Other health risks
themes.text: Waterborne, foodborne, parasitic and other infectious diseases are common, including: bilharzia (schistosomiasis) cholera hepatitis lassa fever rabies tuberculosis typhoid Serious outbreaks sometimes occur. If there is an outbreak of infectious disease, follow the advice of local authorities. To protect yourself from illness: drink boiled water or bottled water with sealed lids avoid ice cubes avoid raw and undercooked food, such as salads don't swim in fresh water, which can expose you to waterborne diseases avoid contact with dogs, cats and other animals Get medical attention straight away if an animal scratches or bites you. Get medical advice if you have a fever or diarrhoea. Ask your doctor about health risks and vaccines before you go. More information: Infectious diseases

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: There’s no agreement between Australia and Guinea for free healthcare. Tourists must pay for all medical care, even if they need to stay in hospital or be evacuated. This can be very expensive. Make sure your travel insurance covers you. The Australian Government won't pay for these costs. Health facilities are limited in Conakry. Outside of the capital, the standard of facilities is poor. You may need to pay cash up-front before doctors and hospitals will treat you, even in an emergency. If you are seriously ill or injured, you'll need to be evacuated. Medical evacuations are extremely expensive. Make sure your travel insurance covers medical evacuations.

themes.heading: Serious crime
themes.text: Serious crimes, such as murder and armed robbery, can attract the death penalty. If you're arrested or detained due process may not be followed and delays should be expected. Pre-trial detention is common and can last for many months. Local prison conditions are harsh, often without regular food and water. The main prison in Conakry suffers from serious overcrowding and security incidents.

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: Penalties for drug offences are severe and include prison sentences in local jails. More information: Carrying or using drugs

themes.heading: LGBTQIA+ information
themes.text: Same-sex relationships are illegal. Fines or jail sentences may apply. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Local customs
themes.text: Dress and behaviour standards are conservative, particularly during Ramadan. Ask locals for advice. Take care not to offend.

themes.heading: Ramadan
themes.text: The Islamic holy month of Ramadan is observed in Guinea. Respect religious and cultural customs and laws at this time. If you're visiting during Ramadan, seek advice about additional considerations from your tour guide, hotel or trusted contacts. During Ramadan, eating, drinking and smoking may be illegal in public during the day. If you're not fasting, avoid these activities around people who are. Seek local advice to avoid offence. Explore our Ramadan page to learn more, including dates for Ramadan.

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office. You could be denied entry to Guinea if you don’t have a return or onward ticket.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Contact an Embassy of the Republic of Guinea for details about visas, currency, customs and quarantine rules.

themes.heading: Border measures
themes.text: Air travel may be cancelled at short notice or borders closed in response to political unrest. Contact your airline for more information. Check with the nearest Guinea Embassy or Guinea Ministry of Health (in French) for details.

themes.heading: Yellow fever vaccination
themes.text: You'll need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Guinea. Some airlines may want to see one when you leave. You may need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to travel to other countries after visiting Guinea. Find out about returning to Australia after exposure to yellow fever. More information: Countries with a risk of yellow fever

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Your passport is valuable and can be used by others to commit crimes. Don’t let anyone trick you into giving it to them. Always keep it in a safe place. If your passport is lost or stolen, tell the Australian Government: In Australia: contact the Australian Passport Information Service. Overseas: contact the nearest Australian embassy or consulate.

themes.heading: Passport with ‘X’ gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The local currency is the Guinean franc (GNF). Banks accept international credit cards and can exchange money. Traveller's cheques and credit cards are only accepted at some major hotels in Conakry. ATMs are unreliable, only dispense small amounts of cash and often don’t accept foreign cards. They should be used only at reputable hotels, stores, and banks. Exchanging currency on the street is illegal. Foreign nationals have been arrested and held in military custody for using unofficial money changers even for small amounts, including at the airport. Outside of the capital, banks are limited and you will likely have to use cash. On departure from Guinea, you cannot take more than 100,000 Guinean francs in cash, or 5,000 US dollars or the equivalent in euros.

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: Road travel can be hazardous, especially during the rainy season from May to October. Roads, even within Conakry, can be flooded and become impassable. Travel after dark is dangerous. Robberies and accidents are common. Criminals posing as police or military, armed with military weapons, have robbed drivers and their passengers at gunpoint, particularly at night. If you plan to arrive in Conakry on a flight after dark, arrange your airport transfer before you travel. The Guinean authorities maintain checkpoints across the country, including on the Conakry airport road. They may ask to search your vehicle and baggage. Officials, or people posing as officials, may try to extort money from foreigners. Carry identification with you at all times. When driving, you must be able to show: vehicle registration hire or ownership records valid driver’s licence proof of road insurance vehicle safety check certificate Carry copies of your documents with you that can be shown instead of your actual passports or identification cards. Driving hazards include: poor driving habits no road or traffic signs poorly maintained vehicles and roads pedestrians and stray animals In the event of an accident, proceed to the nearest police station or medical facility. Roadside assistance and ambulance services are not available. Fuel and water shortages occur regularly affecting transportation. Local authorities may implement emergency measures including closing gas stations across the country or limiting fuel supply with limited notice. Ensure you maintain stores of food, fuel, water and emergency supplies. More information: Driving or riding

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: Guinea has no official public transportation system. Taxis are common, but vehicle and driving standards can be very poor. Avoid the use of moto-taxis and shared taxis/tuk-tuks. Crime occurs in taxis and on long-distance buses, which are often overcrowded and poorly maintained.

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Airlines and airports can restrict what you can take in your luggage. Regulations may be different to those in Australia. Check with your airline before you travel. Check Guinea's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Guinea. The Canadian Embassy in Senegal can give consular assistance to Australians in Guinea, including issuing provisional travel documents.

themes.heading: Canadian Embassy, Dakar
themes.text: Corner Rue Galléni and Rue Briere de l'Isle Dakar, Senegal Phone: +221 33 889 4749 Email: dakar-consular@international.gc.ca Website: www.international.gc.ca/country-pays/senegal/dakar.aspx?lang=eng You can also get consular assistance from the Australian High Commission in Ghana.

themes.heading: Australian High Commission, Accra
themes.text: 2, Second Rangoon Close (cnr Josef Broz Tito Ave) Cantonments Accra, Ghana Phone: +233 302 216 400 Email: AccraHC.Enquiries@dfat.gov.au Website: ghana.highcommission.gov.au Facebook: Australian High Commission, Ghana X: @AusAmbGHA The Australian High Commission in Accra currently has very limited capacity to provide consular assistance in West Africa, except in Ghana. See the High Commission website for details about opening hours and any temporary closures.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas 1300 555 135 within Australia

themes.heading: 기본정보 (basisinformatie)
themes.text: ㅇ 수 도 : 코나크리(Conakry) ㅇ 인 구 : 약 1,475만 명(2024, 세계은행) ㅇ 면 적 : 245,857㎢(한반도와 유사) ㅇ 민족구성 : 풀라니족(Peul), 말링케족(Malinké), 수수족(Susu/Soussou) 등 ㅇ 종 교 : 이슬람교, 기독교 등 ㅇ 언 어 : 프랑스어(공용어), 풀라어(Pular), 말링케어(Malinké), 수수어(Soussou) 등 ㅇ 독 립 : 1958.10.2.(프랑스로부터 독립) ㅇ 화폐단위 : 기니 프랑(Franc guinéen, GNF) / US$1 ≒ 8,765 GNF(2026.6월 기준)

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고 현황
themes.text: [내전, 테러(피랍) 사건 등 동향] ㅇ 기니는 2021.9월 군부 쿠데타 이후 과도기를 거쳐 2025년 헌법 국민투표 및 대통령 선거를 실시하였으나, 정치적 긴장과 사회적 갈등이 완전히 해소되지 않은 상황임. ㅇ 정부 정책, 선거 결과 및 사회·경제 문제 등을 둘러싼 시위가 간헐적으로 발생하고 있으며, 시위 과정에서 도로 봉쇄, 차량 방화, 공공시설 파손 및 치안기관과의 충돌로 인한 인명피해가 발생할 가능성이 있으므로 군중 집결 지역 접근은 자제할 필요가 있음. ㅇ 2014년 기니에서 에볼라가 대규모로 발생하여 2천 명 이상이 사망하였으며, 2021년 2월 에볼라 재발병 사례가 확인된 바 있어 감염병 관련 보건·위생 상황에 지속적인 주의가 필요함. ㅇ 2016.3월 코나크리에서 테러 용의자 2명이 체포되는 등 기니 내 테러 발생 가능성을 완전히 배제할 수는 없으며, 인접 사헬 지역의 불안정한 안보 상황에도 유의할 필요가 있음. ㅇ 2011년 말 우리 기업인이 사업차 기니를 방문 중 피랍되어 일주일 만에 석방된 사례가 있는바, 기니의 풍부한 광물을 미끼로 투자자 또는 구매자를 현지로 유인하여 납치하거나 금전을 편취하는 사례가 발생할 수 있으므로 투자 및 사업 관련 방문 시 각별한 주의가 필요함. ㅇ 말리, 시에라리온, 라이베리아, 코트디부아르 등 접경지역은 치안이 상대적으로 취약하고 무장강도, 납치 및 각종 불법 활동이 발생할 가능성이 있으므로 방문 시 각별한 주의가 필요함. [범죄 현황 등 치안상태] ㅇ 기니의 전반적인 치안은 서아프리카 지역 내에서도 불안정한 편이며, 수도 코나크리를 비롯한 주요 도시에서 소매치기, 오토바이 날치기, 차량털이, 무장강도, 절도 등 각종 범죄가 발생하고 있음. ㅇ 외국인은 범죄의 주요 표적이 될 수 있으며, 공항, 시장, 버스터미널, 환전소, 은행 주변 등 인파가 많은 장소에서 휴대전화, 현금, 가방 등을 노린 범죄가 빈번하게 발생하고 있음. ㅇ 야간에는 범죄 발생 위험이 크게 증가하므로 주·야간을 불문하고 단독 도보 이동은 가급적 자제하고, 특히 야간에는 차량 또는 단체 이동을 권장함. ㅇ 일부 지역에서는 경찰 또는 군인을 사칭하거나 실제 군복·경찰복을 착용한 범죄자에 의한 강도 사건이 보고된 바 있으므로 검문 또는 신분 확인 요구 시 각별한 주의가 필요함. ㅇ 정치적 긴장이 고조되거나 시위가 발생하는 경우 도로 봉쇄, 차량 방화, 상점 약탈, 치안기관과 시위대 간 충돌 등이 발생할 수 있으며, 통신 제한이나 통행금지가 실시될 가능성도 있으므로 시위 및 군중 집결 지역 접근은 자제하는 것이 바람직함. ㅇ 말리 접경지역은 무장강도, 납치, 테러 위험이 존재하는 지역으로 평가되고 있으며, 시에라리온·라이베리아·코트디부아르 접경지역 역시 치안이 상대적으로 취약하므로 방문 시 각별한 주의가 필요함.

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고의 유형
themes.text: ㅇ 관광 중인 여행객에게 잘 아는 사람인 듯 2명이 접근하여 한 명은 말을 걸고 다른 한 명은 주머니 또는 가방에 현금, 물품 등을 강탈하는 범죄가 많이 발생하므로 도보 이동시 주의 필요 ㅇ 출, 입국 시 출입국 경찰들의 현금 요구 사례나 수하물 검사를 일부러 지연시켜 수속에 많은 시간이 소요되도록 하여 금전을 요구하는 사례가 았는 것으로 알려짐 - 출입국시 필요한 서류 준비 및 반입 금지품 등을 사전에 철저히 확인하여 준비하고, 문제 발생시 당황하지 말고 시간이 다소 지체되어도 원칙대로 처리하도록 반응하면서 금전을 주지 않는게 바람직함

themes.heading: 자연재해
themes.text: ㅇ 기니는 대규모 자연재해 발생 빈도가 높은 국가는 아니나, 우기(5월~10월)에는 집중호우로 인한 홍수, 산사태, 도로 유실 및 교통장애가 발생할 수 있음. ㅇ 2025년 8월 코야(Coyah) 주 마네아(Manéah) 지역에서는 집중호우로 인한 산사태가 발생하여 사망자가 발생하는 등 우기철 자연재해 위험이 확인된 바 있음. ㅇ 우기철에는 하천 수위가 급격히 상승할 수 있으므로 강이나 계곡에서의 수영 및 물놀이 활동에 주의가 필요하며, 저지대 및 산사태 우려 지역 방문은 가급적 자제하는 것이 바람직함.

themes.heading: 유의해야 할 지역
themes.text: ㅇ 코나크리 시내 주요 도로, 시장, 버스터미널, 공항 주변 등 인파가 많은 지역에서는 소매치기, 날치기 및 차량털이 피해에 유의해야 함. ㅇ 도보 이동은 주·야간을 불문하고 가급적 자제하고, 이동 시에는 차량을 이용하거나 단체로 이동하는 것이 바람직함. 특히 손가방, 작은 핸드백 등 소형 가방은 범죄 표적이 될 수 있으므로 소지품 관리에 각별히 유의해야 함. ㅇ 야간에는 인적이 드문 지역, 해안가 및 외곽지역 방문을 자제하고, 부득이하게 이동할 경우 신뢰할 수 있는 현지인 또는 숙소 관계자와 동행하며 가급적 차량을 이용하는 것이 바람직함. ㅇ 야간에 경찰 또는 군인을 사칭한 사람이 숙소 출입을 요구하거나 검문을 이유로 접근하는 사례가 있을 수 있으므로, 신원이 확실히 확인되지 않은 사람에게는 문을 열어주지 말고 숙소 관계자 또는 지인에게 먼저 도움을 요청하는 것이 바람직함. ㅇ 말리, 시에라리온, 라이베리아, 코트디부아르 등 접경지역은 치안이 상대적으로 취약하고 무장강도, 납치 및 각종 불법 활동이 발생할 가능성이 있으므로 방문 시 각별한 주의가 필요함.

themes.heading: 출입국 (in- en uitreis)
themes.text: 무비자 입국 가능 여부(1회 입국시 최대 연속 체류 가능 일자) ㅇ 외교관 여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 관용여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 일반여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 도착사증 발급 여부 : 도착사증 미발급 ㅇ 전자비자(e-Visa) 발급 여부 : 사전 신청 가능 - 기니 입국 전 기니 국경관리경찰 공식 전자비자 사이트(paf.gov.gn/visa)를 통해 전자비자(e-Visa)를 사전에 신청·승인받아야 함. - 전자비자 승인 후 입국 시 승인서, 여권, 황열 예방접종증명서 등 필요 서류를 제시해야 하며, 사증 없이 도착할 경우 입국이 거부될 수 있음. 입국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 여권은 입국일 기준 잔여 유효기간이 충분히 남아 있어야 하며, 입국 목적에 맞는 비자를 사전에 준비해야 함. ㅇ 기니는 황열 위험국가로 분류되어 있어 입국 시 황열 예방접종증명서를 요구할 수 있으므로 사전에 준비하는 것이 바람직함. ㅇ 외환 반입에는 제한이 없으나, 도착 시 반입 금액을 세관에 신고해야 함. 출국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 출국 시 외화는 입국 당시 신고한 금액 범위 내에서 반출 가능함. ㅇ 기니 프랑(Guinean Franc, GNF) 등 현지화폐의 국외 반출은 금지 또는 제한될 수 있으므로 출국 전 사용 또는 환전 필요. 참고자료 ㅇ 해당 국가의 주한공관홈페이지 : 주한공관 없음 - 우리나라 겸임 주일 기니 대사관 홈페이지 : ambaguitokyo.org ㅇ 해당 국가의 한국 대사관 홈페이지 : 기니에는 우리대사관이 없으며, 주세네갈대한민국대사관이 겸임 - 주세네갈 대한민국 대사관 : https://overseas.mofa.go.kr/sn-ko/index.do ㅇ 기타 참고자료 - 해당 국가의 관광청 : tourisme.gov.gn

themes.heading: 팁 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 기니에서 팁은 의무사항은 아니나, 호텔, 식당, 운전기사, 짐 운반 서비스 등에서는 외국인에게 팁을 기대하는 경우가 있음. ㅇ 팁 지급 여부와 금액은 서비스 만족도에 따라 자율적으로 판단하되, 이용 전 요금 포함 여부를 확인하는 것이 바람직함. 복장 및 생활 문화 ㅇ 기니는 이슬람 문화의 영향이 큰 국가로, 현지에서는 비교적 단정한 복장을 선호하는 경향이 있음. ㅇ 외국인의 복장을 엄격히 제한하는 분위기는 아니나, 관공서, 시장, 종교시설, 지방 지역 방문 시에는 노출이 심한 복장을 피하는 것이 바람직함. 종교 관련 ㅇ 기니는 국민 다수가 이슬람교를 믿고 있으나, 기독교 및 토착신앙도 공존하고 있음. ㅇ 종교 관련 발언에는 신중을 기하고, 이슬람교 또는 현지 종교 관습에 대한 비판적 발언이나 논쟁은 피하는 것이 바람직함. ㅇ 라마단 기간에는 공공장소에서의 음식 섭취, 흡연, 음주 등에 대해 현지 정서를 배려할 필요가 있음. 촬영 관련 ㅇ 기니에서는 군사시설, 경찰·헌병 시설, 정부청사, 대통령 관저, 공항, 항만, 교량 등 주요 국가시설 및 전략시설에 대한 사진·영상 촬영이 금지되거나 엄격히 제한될 수 있음. ㅇ 위 시설을 촬영할 경우 체포, 조사, 장비 압수 등 문제가 발생할 수 있으므로 촬영을 삼가야 함. ㅇ 군인, 경찰 등 제복 착용자나 현지인을 촬영할 경우에도 사전에 동의를 구하는 것이 바람직함.

themes.heading: 주요 교통 법규 및 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 기니는 도로 상태가 전반적으로 열악하고, 비포장 또는 부분 포장 도로가 많아 운전 시 각별한 주의가 필요함. ㅇ 노후 차량, 불충분한 도로 표지, 야간 조명 부족, 보행자·가축의 도로 진입 등으로 교통사고 위험이 높음. ㅇ 우기(5월~10월)에는 집중호우와 침수로 일부 도로가 통행 불가능해질 수 있으며, 교량 파손 및 도로 유실 가능성도 있음. ㅇ 코나크리 시내에서는 교통체증이 심하고, 오토바이·택시·미니버스의 급정차, 역주행, 차선 변경 등으로 사고 위험이 높으므로 방어운전이 필요함. ㅇ 주요 도로와 지방도로에는 경찰·군 검문소가 운영되는 경우가 있으며, 검문 시 운전면허증, 차량 등록서류, 보험증 등 관련 서류를 요구할 수 있음. ㅇ 야간에는 도로 상태 확인이 어렵고 범죄 및 검문 관련 위험이 증가하므로, 단기 방문자는 직접 운전과 야간 운전을 가급적 자제하는 것이 바람직함. ㅇ 국제운전면허증으로 운전 가능하나, 장기 체류자는 현지 운전면허 전환 요건을 사전에 확인할 필요가 있음.

themes.heading: 대중교통
themes.text: ㅇ 기니의 대중교통은 전반적으로 안전성과 신뢰성이 낮은 편이며, 차량 노후화, 과밀 운행, 안전장치 미흡 등의 문제가 있음. ㅇ 대형버스 및 미니버스는 노선·정류장 표시가 명확하지 않고, 혼잡도와 치안상 위험이 있어 외국인 여행객의 이용은 권장되지 않음. ㅇ 택시는 외국인이 비교적 많이 이용하는 교통수단이나, 요금제가 명확하지 않은 경우가 많으므로 탑승 전 목적지와 요금을 반드시 협의해야 함. ㅇ 택시 차량 상태가 좋지 않은 경우가 많고, 급정차·급출발·무리한 차선 변경 등이 빈번할 수 있으므로 이용 시 주의가 필요함. ㅇ 가능하면 호텔 또는 신뢰할 수 있는 업체를 통해 운전기사가 포함된 차량을 이용하는 것이 바람직함.

themes.heading: 날씨
themes.text: ㅇ 기니는 열대 기후로, 건기(11월~4월)와 우기(5월~10월)로 구분됨. ㅇ 연중 기온은 대체로 22~32℃ 수준이나, 우기에는 고온다습한 날씨가 지속되고 집중호우로 인한 침수, 도로 유실 및 교통장애가 발생할 수 있음. ㅇ 우기에는 하천 수위가 급격히 상승할 수 있으므로 강이나 계곡에서의 수영 및 물놀이 활동은 자제하는 것이 바람직함.

themes.heading: 감염병
themes.text: ㅇ 기니에서는 말라리아, 황열, 콜레라, 라싸열, 뎅기열, 홍역 등 감염병 발생 위험이 있으므로 출국 전 예방접종 및 예방수칙 확인이 필요함. ㅇ 기니 입국 시 황열 예방접종증명서를 요구할 수 있으므로 사전에 준비하는 것이 바람직함. ㅇ 모기 매개 질병 예방을 위해 모기기피제 사용, 긴 옷 착용, 모기장 이용 등 모기물림 예방수칙을 준수해야 함. ㅇ 음식물 및 수인성 질병 예방을 위해 생수 또는 병입수를 음용하고, 위생이 불확실한 음식 섭취는 자제하는 것이 바람직함. ㅇ 기니 내 감염병 발생 현황은 기니 국가보건안전청(ANSS, Agence Nationale de Sécurité Sanitaire) 및 기니 보건위생부(Ministère de la Santé et de l’Hygiène Publique) 발표자료를 통해 확인할 수 있음.

themes.heading: Country summary
themes.text: There were no changes to the advisory level or indicators. The advisory summary was updated. Exercise increased caution in Guinea due to crime, unrest, and inadequate health infrastructure. Advisory summary Restrictions on U.S. Government Personnel Movement U.S. government employees working in Guinea are prohibited from traveling between cities at night due to safety risks. Crime Crimes of opportunity are common in Guinea, especially in Conakry. Typical incidents include pickpocketing, purse snatching, theft from vehicles, assaults, and residential burglaries. Travelers should be especially alert in crowded areas, such as markets. Unrest Demonstrations and protests have occurred without warning in Guinea and sometimes result in violence. These events can develop around political issues, large events, or holidays. Security forces may use tear gas and gunfire to disperse crowds. Demonstrations may disrupt transportation and essential services. Health Guinea’s health infrastructure is limited. Access to necessary healthcare may not be available. Health services, hygiene, and quality control do not meet U.S. standards of care. Local drinking water is unsafe. Pharmacies are not well regulated, and locally available medications may be unsafe. If you decide to travel to Guinea: Review our information on Crime Abroad and for Victims of Crime. Do not display signs of wealth, like expensive watches, jewelry, or phones. Avoid demonstrations and crowds. Stay aware of your surroundings at all times. Exercise caution when walking or driving at night. Do not physically resist any robbery attempt. Stay alert when visiting banks or ATMs. Keep travel documents up to date and easily accessible. Ask permission prior to taking pictures of government buildings. Check with your doctor about required vaccines and shots for high-risk exposure before you go to Guinea. Review the Health section in the Travel Guidance for Guinea. Enroll in the Smart Traveler Enrollment Program (STEP) to get important updates and alerts from the U.S. embassy or consulate. Enrolling helps the U.S. embassy or consulate contact you or your emergency contact in an emergency. Review the Country Security Report for Guinea. Visit the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) website for the latest Travel Health Information for Guinea. Whether you’re a first time or frequent traveler, use the International Travel Checklist. We highly recommend that you buy travel insurance before you travel. Check with your travel insurance provider about evacuation assistance, medical insurance, and trip cancelation coverage.
